summary: This post provides a comprehensive wrap-up of dtrace.conf(16), a quadrennial unconference dedicated to DTrace, the dynamic tracing framework. Cantrill surveys the day's presentations covering core D language improvements, practical DTrace usage across various languages and systems (Ruby, Go, Postgres), security research on DTrace exploitation, and the launch of OpenDTrace for cross-platform collaboration. The conference brought together an eclectic mix of technologists from academia, industry giants like Bloomberg, and the Joyent engineering team to share advances in systems observability.
key_points: DTrace is receiving significant language improvements including if/else support, syntactic sugar for maintainability, and user-level CTF understanding
Go's lack of frame pointer preservation has hindered DTrace integration, but this is being fixed in Go 1.7
Bloomberg faces extreme instrumentation challenges with binaries containing multiple languages including Fortran 77 and millions of symbols
OpenDTrace initiative launched to encourage cross-platform development and collaboration, mirroring the OpenZFS effort
DTrace security research revealed exploitation possibilities, presented strategically before drinks
money_quotes: A singular point of pride for me personally is how much DTrace was used to implement and debug ZFS: Matt has used DTrace as much as anyone, and anything that he feels he needs to make his life easier is something that will almost certainly benefit all DTrace users.
DTrace itself is entirely open source, so 'OpenDTrace' is something of a redundancy.

Just to save you the time filling out your Cantrill Presentation Bingo Card: you can find me punching myself at 16:19, me offering unsolicited personal medical history at 20:11, and me getting trolled by unikernels at 38:25.
key_insight: The DTrace community continues to evolve the tool through language improvements, cross-platform collaboration via OpenDTrace, and expanding instrumentation capabilities across modern languages and massive enterprise systems.
